---
title: "Myrtle Oak (Quercus myrtifolia)"
slug: "quercus-myrtifolia"
group: "Plantae"
image: "https://static.inaturalist.org/photos/622605173/medium.jpg"        # keep for thumbnails
image-alt: "Myrtle Oak"  # keep for a11y
tax_kingdom: "Plantae"
tax_class: "Magnoliopsida"
tax_order: "Fagales"
tax_family: "Fagaceae"
categories: ["Plantae", "Magnoliopsida", "Fagales", "Fagaceae"]   # these drive the chips + filtering
freeze: true
---

<figure class="species-figure">

<img src="https://static.inaturalist.org/photos/622605173/medium.jpg" alt="Myrtle Oak" class="species-photo"/>

<figcaption class="photo-credit">

Photo: <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/people/stevecollins">
stevecollins</a> (CC BY-NC) via <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/341948377">iNaturalist</a>

</figcaption>

</figure>

## Taxonomy

-   **Class:** Magnoliopsida
-   **Order:** Fagales
-   **Family:** Fagaceae

## Occurrence at DeLuca Preserve

-   [DeLuca Bio Blitz Species Observations](https://www.inaturalist.org/observations?project_id=191727&subview=map&taxon_id=167660)

## Conservation & rarity context

-   IUCN: Not Listed
-   **Rarity note:** Common species in Florida found in dry, sandy habitats.

## Natural history notes

A small, evergreen oak characterized by its thick, glossy, holly-like leaves and shrubby to low tree growth form. In Florida, it is commonly found in dry, sandy habitats such as scrub, dunes, and coastal uplands. It produces acorns that serve as a food source for wildlife and is a characteristic species of fire-adapted scrub ecosystems.

## References / links

-   [iNaturalist species page](https://www.inaturalist.org/taxa/167660-Quercus-myrtifolia)
-   [Local profile](https://florida.plantatlas.usf.edu/plant/species/3910)
